next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

The weather alert hit all their phones within the same half minute, cheap screens buzzing on the dresser, on the milk crate, beside Kerene's folding cot. Micah opened one eye to the sound and saw the room already awake around him. The morning light had gone the color of dishwater. Even before he stood up he could feel what the air was doing: it wasn't moving, not exactly. It was holding itself back.

On the TV balanced crooked on top of the little fridge, a woman with hurricane hair and perfect teeth stood in front of a map of South Florida and talked about watches, feeder bands, storm surge, all the ways water could stop being ordinary. Marcia was in the kitchenette writing a list on the back of a church envelope. Her handwriting got tighter when she was angry; this morning it looked like she was trying to cut the paper.

"Water, bread, batteries," she said, mostly to herself. "Candles if the price isn't stupid."

Tiana stood by the bed they called hers though it had stopped being only hers weeks ago, pulling her document folder up from the floor and stacking it on top of her jeans. She had on one of Malik's old T-shirts, big enough to make her look younger until she lifted her face and the room remembered not to mistake her for a child.

Kerene sat on her cot in bicycle shorts and a bleach-white tank top, tying a satin scarf over her hair with the speed of somebody who had learned to make herself presentable in bus-stop glass. One hoop earring was missing, so the other one flashed by itself when she turned. "Miami people love a panic," she said. "You'll see....
open_hooks_to_carry: Delroy's hidden support for Devon complicates him rather than excuses him.
Marcia's rage carries class fury, maternal fury, and betrayal.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...t ever being discussed in it.

Tiana could keep the receipts. She could carry them in her own folder like ammunition. She could leave the nursing letter out where Marcia would have to see it and explain. Instead she put the nursing packet back where she had found it, under the certificates and old school forms, and slid the receipts into their check-cashing envelope before laying them flat at the bottom of the trunk.

It wasn't forgiveness or mercy. It was storage.

She closed the lid softly and turned the key.

When she stood, the room seemed no larger, the air no cooler. Nothing had been solved except what papers needed to go where. But the file was alive now. Miss Gumbs had been right about that much. Alive was not glamorous. Alive was a stack clipped together with a black binder clip borrowed from Marcia's church notebook, a tomorrow full of copies and passport photos and a printer that might or might not work, a future small enough to fit under her arm and still heavy.

Tiana slid the packet into her folder and pushed it under her pillow on the couch before lying down. Across the room Kerene was already asleep on the folding cot, one hand flung over her head, the freezer bag with the passport peeking from under the pillowcase like a ridiculous, necessary secret. Marcia and Delroy moved quietly in the kitchenette, not touching, the space between them full of things Tiana could almost name now and still did not fully understand.

The folder edge pressed into her cheek through the pillow, paper instead of softness and proof instead of comfort.

She closed her eyes anyway.
state_deltas_required: Keep Claude from humiliating Delroy in front of the children and salvage some working version of marriage.
Delroy leaves after the fight, and the family can no longer pretend its economy was honest.
